title: approve_transfer is permissionless — any caller can invoke with any Request
description: `approve_transfer<T>` is `public` and takes only `&Treasury`, `&mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>`, and `&Version` — no Auth proof. Any on-chain caller that holds a legitimate `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` hot potato can invoke the function. This is by PAS design (autoresolve is permissionless — the Policy's required-approval set gates what actually resolves), but it means the module-internal whitelist checks are the ONLY compliance barrier at this call-site.
impact: If any one of the whitelist checks has a bug (e.g. a case where `assert_can_send/receive` does not fire), compliance is bypassed. Defense in depth is single-layer here. Call-site reviewers must treat the whitelist assertions as audit-critical.
recommendation: (1) Add a code comment to the module explicitly stating that `approve_transfer` is the sole whitelist enforcement point. (2) Add tests exercising every combination of sender-not-whitelisted / recipient-not-whitelisted / paused / amount-zero. (3) Consider wrapping the four asserts in a named helper `assert_transfer_allowed<T>(treasury, sender, recipient, amount)` to avoid drift.

title: WhitelistManagePermission auth does NOT bind to a PAS-registered coin
description: The four whitelist functions take `_: &Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `auth::new_auth` creates that proof after asserting `treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()` (i.e. a TreasuryCap DOF exists) — but it does NOT assert `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()`. An operator with WhitelistManagePermission for a role attached to a regulated (non-PAS) coin T could mint the proof and attempt whitelist mutations, aborting deep in DOF borrow_mut for a missing whitelist DOF.
impact: User-facing error message will be a framework-level 'dynamic field not found' rather than a Hadron-defined error. Debugging burden shifts to the operator. No safety bypass — the DOF simply doesn't exist.
recommendation: In each of the four `pas_admin` whitelist functions, add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` before calling the treasury mutator. The `ENotPasCoin` error already exists in treasury.move (line 67).

title: pas_supply::seize can move tokens to a non-whitelisted recipient when source is seized
description: `seize<T>` asserts `treasury.assert_can_receive<T>(to.owner())` — the destination account must be whitelisted to receive. It does NOT check the source. This is deliberate (seize is meant to extract from de-whitelisted / sanctioned addresses), but surfaces the semantic: the seizer MUST already have whitelisted some holding account to be the target of seized funds.
impact: None on safety. Potentially confusing if a regulator assumes seize funds can go anywhere. Document.
recommendation: Add a module-level comment explaining the asymmetry: 'seize extracts from de-whitelisted source; destination MUST be whitelisted by compliance'. Matches the Notion spec.

title: ClawbackApproval<T> witness is `drop`-only; can be freely constructed in-module
description: `ClawbackApproval<T>` is `public struct ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop`. Anyone inside `hadron::pas_supply` can construct it via `ClawbackApproval<T>()` to satisfy the Policy's required-approval. The module's own access control (MintPermission / RedeemPermission / SeizePermission) is thus the only gate. This is the PAS-intended pattern (witness + module-scoping), so it's fine — but the observation needs to be stated explicitly for audit.
impact: No bypass. Just a reminder that the safety of burn/seize depends entirely on the Auth checks, not the witness.
recommendation: Add a docstring on `ClawbackApproval<T>` noting 'only constructible inside this module; module-private use of burn/seize guards the witness'.
evidence: public struct ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop;

title: TransferApproval<T> witness is `drop`-only; same observation as ClawbackApproval
description: `TransferApproval<T>` is `public struct TransferApproval<phantom T>() has drop`. Same pattern as ClawbackApproval. `approve_transfer` (public, permissionless) constructs it after the whitelist checks. This is secure because (a) the witness is scoped to this module, and (b) the function enforces the checks before constructing. Surface for explicit audit acknowledgment.
impact: No bypass. Just documents the trust chain.
recommendation: Add a docstring noting the witness is only constructed by `approve_transfer` after whitelist + version + pause + amount>0 checks pass.
evidence: public struct TransferApproval<phantom T>() has drop;

title: pas_supply::burn does not require source account to be whitelisted
description: `burn<T>` only checks `!is_mint_redeem_paused<T>` and `amount > 0` — not that the source (`request.data().owner()`) is or is not whitelisted. By design, burn goes through a prior `account::clawback_balance<T>` which requires no auth (it's admin-action-from-on-chain; see PAS account.move clawback_balance L95). So burn is a clawback → TreasuryCap burn. Safety comes from RedeemPermission gating.
impact: None — by design, burn is an admin flow that extracts supply regardless of whitelist state.
recommendation: Document in the module doc that burn extracts supply unilaterally once `RedeemPermission` is granted, and does not require the source to be whitelisted.

title: add_to_whitelist default has both can_send AND can_receive true
description: The package-internal helper `add_to_whitelist<T>` hard-codes `WhitelistEntry { can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. An operator who wants to add a receive-only (custody) or send-only (distribution) address must first call `add` and then immediately `set_whitelist_send(addr, false)` — a two-step dance that leaves an intermediate state where the address has both permissions.
impact: Operational gap. A PTB built by an operator can atomically do both steps, but a script that does them in separate transactions exposes a fleeting window of over-permission. For institutional bond tokens this is unlikely to be exploited, but the default-true bias is worth flagging.
recommendation: Offer a typed helper: `add_to_whitelist_with_perms<T>(treasury, addr, can_send: bool, can_receive: bool)` — forward the existing default to keep backward compat. Expose via `pas_admin` as a second function.

title: Whitelist DOF stored via dynamic_object_field but Table is not an object
description: The whitelist `Table<address, WhitelistEntry>` is stored as a `dynamic_object_field` (DOF). `Table` is an owned `store` struct but it is NOT a `key`-abilities object — storing it via `dynamic_object_field::add` requires the value to have `key + store`. Confirm against the Sui framework: `sui::table::Table<K, V>` IS `has key, store` (it IS a first-class object with a UID). So storing via DOF is correct. Surfacing for cross-check.
impact: None if DOF is correct. If it were DF instead, the Table would not have a derived address — it matters for framework introspection, not correctness here.
recommendation: Confirm that using DOF (not DF) is the intended storage for the whitelist, given each Table is a shared-by-parent first-class object. Document.

title: ENotPasCoin exists but no public function checks it on whitelist reads
description: The error `ENotPasCoin` exists (line 67). `policy_cap<T>` asserts it via `is_pas_coin<T>` (line 273). But the whitelist read helpers (`is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>`, `assert_can_send<T>`, `assert_can_receive<T>`) do NOT check `is_pas_coin<T>` before borrowing the whitelist DOF. For a non-PAS coin T, the borrow aborts at framework-level.
impact: Same as R1-003, R2-002. Worth emphasizing at the treasury layer where the DOFs actually live.
recommendation: Add `assert!(is_pas_coin<T>(treasury), ENotPasCoin);` as the first line of each of the five whitelist-read helpers. Then `pas_admin` callers inherit the guard.

title: Move.toml edition is `2024.beta` — should be `2024`
description: `edition = "2024.beta"` was used during the 2024 edition preview. Per the globally-adopted convention and Mysten CLAUDE.md, Move packages should declare `edition = "2024"` after the preview period ended.
impact: Low. `2024.beta` may still be accepted by the current toolchain but may be deprecated later.
recommendation: Change to `edition = "2024"`. Also update `token_template_pas/Move.toml` and `token_template/Move.toml` if they carry the same value.

title: pas_supply::mint consumes Auth by reference but burn / seize take `mut request`
description: `mint<T>(.. _: &Auth<MintPermission, T>, account: &Account, ..)` takes `&Account` (immutable), but PAS `account::deposit_balance<C>` takes `&Account` too. That's fine. Observation: consistency across the three functions (mint/burn/seize) in their `mut`-ness is good.

title: approve_transfer emits event AFTER approve — event-before-resolve ordering
description: `approve_transfer` emits `PasTransferApproved` immediately after `request.approve(TransferApproval<T>())`. The actual transfer resolves elsewhere (client-side PTB calls `send_funds::resolve_balance` after this). If the PTB aborts between `approve_transfer` and `resolve_balance`, the event fires but the transfer does NOT complete.
impact: An off-chain indexer seeing `PasTransferApproved` cannot conclude the transfer completed. Must correlate with `SendFunds` upstream events or observe on-chain balance changes.
recommendation: Document in the event's docstring that it indicates the approval step only, not final settlement. Consider renaming the event to `PasTransferApprovalIssued` to make it semantically distinct.

title: full-setup.ts uses process.env.SIGNER_1_PK! non-null assertion without validation
description: `const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;` uses the `!` non-null assertion. If the env var is unset, `pk1B64` is `undefined` and `fromBase64(undefined)` throws a runtime error. No early validation with a helpful message.
impact: Example script fails with a cryptic error. Not a production concern but hurts developer onboarding.
recommendation: Guard: `if (!process.env.SIGNER_1_PK) throw new Error('SIGNER_1_PK env var required for full-setup example');`. Do the same for any other non-null-asserted env var in this file.
evidence:
const pk1B64 = process.env.SIGNER_1_PK!;
    const receiver = new Ed25519PublicKey(fromBase64(pk1B64).slice(1)).toSuiAddress();

title: deployPasTokenTemplate.ts hard-codes dependencies [0x1, 0x2] — misses any 3rd-party stdlib
description: The publish transaction declares dependencies as `[0x1, 0x2]` only. Since the PAS token template only uses `sui::coin` and `sui::coin_registry`, this is sufficient today. If future template changes use the PAS framework directly, this list must be extended.
recommendation: Add an inline comment explaining why only 0x1/0x2 are needed; cite the module's uses.

title: register_transfer_template writes with type_name::with_defining_ids — consistent with PAS templates
description: `register_transfer_template` keys the template by `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>()`. Upstream `pas::templates::set_template_command<A>` keys by `type_name::with_defining_ids<A>()` (`/Users/alilloig/workspace/pas/packages/pas/sources/templates.move` line 31). Keys match. Good.
recommendation: No action. Noted for audit record.
